title: Democratic Features Index
subtitle: Data by the [Bertelsmann Transformation Index](#dod:bertelsmann-transformation-index). Expert estimates of the extent of political participation, the rule of law, stable democratic institutions, political and social integration, and a capable state. The index ranges from 1 to 10 (most democratic).
note: Data excludes long-term members of the OECD because they are considered stable democracies.
caption:
Democratic Features Index

Data by the Bertelsmann Transformation Index. Expert estimates of the extent of political participation, the rule of law, stable democratic institutions, political and social integration, and a capable state. The index ranges from 1 to 10 (most democratic).

This choropleth world map displays the Democratic Features Index for 2025, based on expert estimates of political participation, rule of law, institutional stability, integration, and state capacity. The index ranges from 1 to 10, with 10 representing the most democratic. The legend uses a gradient from white (1) to dark blue (10), with tick marks at each integer. Long-term OECD members are excluded from the dataset and appear with diagonal hatching; this includes the United States, Canada, most of Western Europe, Australia, New Zealand, Japan, and South Korea. Among the countries shown, Uruguay and Costa Rica sit in the darkest blue band, indicating scores near 9 or 10. Chile, Argentina, Brazil, India, Mongolia, and Botswana appear in mid-to-dark blue shades, placing them roughly between 6 and 8. Mexico, Colombia, Peru, Indonesia, and South Africa fall in lighter blue tones, suggesting scores between 4 and 6. Much of Sub-Saharan Africa, parts of Central Asia, and several Middle Eastern countries appear in very light blue or near-white shades, corresponding to scores between 1 and 3. Russia and China are rendered in pale lavender, placing them in the lower half of the scale. The visual distribution shows higher scores concentrated in parts of Latin America and select Asian and African nations, while lower scores dominate large swaths of Africa, Central Asia, and the Middle East.
